conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives with me. I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! I can see you have a son named Alex who is 17 years old and lives with you.

Let me ask you a few more questions to assess your eligibility:

**Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?** (For example, is he in school, sixth form, or college studying for A-levels, Scottish Highers, or equivalent qualifications?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in education at the moment. He finished school but he's not doing A-levels or anything like that right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me gather a bit more detail to assess your eligibility properly.

**Is Alex currently registered with a careers service or Connexions service, and has he been accepted for approved training that is due to start soon?** (This would apply if he's between educational courses or waiting to start an apprenticeship or training programme.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with the careers service or anything like that. He's just finished school and is at home at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Before I can make a final determination, let me just ask a couple more questions:

**Is Alex registered for work, seeking work, or currently working? And does he receive any benefits in his own name, such as Universal Credit or Jobseeker's Allowance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's not really doing much at the moment - he's not working or looking for work formally. And no, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few final questions to complete the assessment:

1. **Are you responsible for Alex?** (This means you live with him and are financially supporting him)
2. **Are you or your partner currently living or working in the UK?**
3. **Is there anyone else who might also be claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm responsible for him - he lives here with me and I support him. Yes, I live in the UK, in Manchester actually. And no, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me just read the official guidance to ensure I give you an accurate assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for your patience. Based on the information you've provided and the official UK government guidance, I now have enough information to make a determination about your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: While you meet the general requirements (you're responsible for Alex, he lives with you, you're in the UK, and no one else is claiming), Alex is 17 years old and not in approved education or training. 

According to the UK government rules:
- Child Benefit normally covers children under 16
- For young people aged 16-19, Child Benefit can continue **only if** they remain in approved education or training (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or equivalent qualifications)

Since Alex has finished school and is not currently doing A-levels or any other approved education or training, he doesn't meet the criteria for continued Child Benefit.

**However**, if Alex's circumstances change - for example, if he decides to go back into full-time education, starts college, or registers with a careers service for approved training - you may become eligible at that point. You could reapply for Child Benefit if that happens.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: According to the UK government rules, Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16. For children aged 16 or over (up to age 20), you can still qualify BUT only if they stay in approved education or training. Alex is 17 years old, has finished school, and is not currently in any approved education or training (not doing A-levels or equivalent qualifications). He has also not registered with a careers service or been accepted for approved training. Since Alex is over 16 and not in approved education or training, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. The government guidance clearly states: 'You'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child: starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training' - and while Alex isn't working, the key requirement is that children over 16 must be in approved education or training to continue qualifying.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for their 17-year-old son Alex who lives with them in the UK. While the claimant meets the general requirements (responsible for the child, lives in the UK, no one else claiming), Alex does not meet the age-related education requirement. Since Alex is over 16 and not in approved education or training, Child Benefit is not available.

Alex is 17 years old, has finished school, and is not currently in any approved education or training (not doing A-levels or equivalent qualifications). He has also not registered with a careers service or been accepted for approved training. Since Alex is over 16 and not in approved education or training, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. The government guidance clearly states: 'You'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child: starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training' - and while Alex isn't working, the key requirement is that children over 16 must be in approved education or training to continue qualifying.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for their 17-year-old son Alex who lives with them in the UK. While the claimant meets the general requirements (responsible for the child, lives in the UK, no one else claiming), Alex does not meet the age-related education requirement. Since Alex is over 16 and not in approved education or training, Child Benefit is not available.
